message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To estimate associations between COVID-19 vaccination and Long Covid symptoms in adults who were infected with SARS-CoV-2 prior to vaccination.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Observational cohort study using individual-level interrupted time series analysis.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Random sample from the community population of the UK.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>28,356 COVID-19 Infection Survey participants (mean age 46 years, 56% female, 89% white) aged 18 to 69 years who received at least their first vaccination after test-confirmed infection.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Presence of long Covid symptoms at least 12 weeks after infection over the follow-up period 3 February to 5 September 2021.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Median follow-up was 141 days from first vaccination (among all participants) and 67 days from second vaccination (84% of participants). First vaccination was associated with an initial 12.8% decrease (95% confidence interval: −18.6% to −6.6%) in the odds of Long Covid, but increasing by 0.3% (−0.6% to +1.2%) per week after the first dose. Second vaccination was associated with an 8.8% decrease (−14.1% to −3.1%) in the odds of Long Covid, with the odds subsequently decreasing by 0.8% (−1.2% to −0.4%) per week. There was no statistical evidence of heterogeneity in associations between vaccination and Long Covid by socio-demographic characteristics, health status, whether hospitalised with acute COVID-19, vaccine type (adenovirus vector or mRNA), or duration from infection to vaccination.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The likelihood of Long Covid symptoms reduced after COVID-19 vaccination, and the improvement was sustained over the follow-up period after the second dose. Vaccination may contribute to a reduction in the population health burden of Long Covid, though longer follow-up time is needed.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Summary box</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>What is already known on this topic</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>COVID-19 vaccines are effective at reducing rates of SARS-CoV-2 infection, transmission, hospitalisation, and death</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The incidence of Long Covid may be reduced if infected after vaccination, but the relationship between vaccination and pre-existing long COVID symptoms is unclear, as published studies are generally small and with self-selected participants</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                  <jats:p>What this study adds</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The likelihood of Long Covid symptoms reduced after COVID-19 vaccination, and the improvement was sustained over the follow-up period after the second dose</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>There was no evidence of differences in this relationship by socio-demographic characteristics, health-related factors, vaccine type, or duration from infection to vaccination</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Although causality cannot be inferred from this observational evidence, vaccination may contribute to a reduction in the population health burden of Long Covid; further research is needed to understand the biological mechanisms that may ultimately contribute to the development of therapeutics for Long Covid</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Changes in the trajectory of Long Covid symptoms following COVID-19 vaccination: community-based cohort study
